title: Extract shared constraint reporting helper from cmd_check and cmd_build
analysis: cmd_check (lines 93-106) and cmd_build (lines 170-183) contain identical constraint-reporting loops: iterate ConstraintCheckEntry slice, match Satisfaction to status string, format label (with id fallback), println each line, and track all_satisfied. Both use println! (stdout). The extraction is a pure refactoring — no behavior changes. The function should accept a &mut impl Write for unit-testability while both callers pass stdout(). ConstraintCheckEntry is pub from reify_eval; Satisfaction is pub from reify_types; both already depend on these crates. Existing integration tests in cli_check.rs and cli_build.rs already assert on stdout output format ('OK', 'VIOLATED', 'All constraints satisfied', 'Some constraints violated') and exit codes, providing full regression coverage for the refactoring.

steps.description: Add #[cfg(test)] mod tests to main.rs with unit tests for report_constraint_results: (1) returns true and formats 'OK' for all-satisfied entries, (2) returns false and formats 'VIOLATED' for violated entries, (3) formats 'INDETERMINATE' correctly, (4) uses entry.id Display as fallback when label is None, (5) uses label when present. Tests construct ConstraintCheckEntry values with ConstraintNodeId::new and write to Vec<u8> buffer.

Add report_constraint_results(results: &[ConstraintCheckEntry], out: &mut impl std::io::Write) -> bool function to main.rs. It iterates the slice, matches entry.satisfaction to status string (OK/VIOLATED/INDETERMINATE), sets all_satisfied=false on Violated, formats label with id fallback, writes '  {status} {label}' lines via writeln!, and returns all_satisfied. Add 'use reify_eval::ConstraintCheckEntry;' and 'use std::io::Write;' imports.

Replace the duplicated loop in cmd_check (lines 93-106) with: let all_satisfied = report_constraint_results(&result.constraint_results, &mut std::io::stdout()); Replace the duplicated loop in cmd_build (lines 170-183) with the same call. Remove the now-unused 'use reify_types::Satisfaction' from the top-level import if it's no longer referenced directly (it's still used inside the helper, but the helper accesses it via the ConstraintCheckEntry field, so check if Satisfaction is used elsewhere in main.rs).
Run existing integration tests (cli_check.rs, cli_build.rs) and the new unit tests to verify no regressions. All tests in crates/reify-cli must pass: cargo test -p reify-cli.

design_decisions.decision: Use &mut impl Write parameter instead of hardcoding println!
design_decisions.rationale: Makes the function unit-testable with Vec<u8> buffers. Both callers pass &mut std::io::stdout(). Minimal overhead, standard Rust pattern for testable output.

design_decisions.decision: Single combined function rather than separate all_satisfied + format functions
design_decisions.rationale: The task description suggests either approach. A single function matches the existing code shape (one loop does both checking and printing) and minimizes the diff. The loop naturally needs to inspect each entry for both purposes anyway.

design_decisions.decision: Keep Satisfaction import in top-level scope
design_decisions.rationale: The report_constraint_results helper uses Satisfaction variants directly in its match arm, so the import is still needed at module scope. The helper is a module-level function in main.rs, not a method.

design_decisions.decision: Unit tests in main.rs #[cfg(test)] rather than a new file
design_decisions.rationale: Standard Rust convention for unit-testing private/module-level functions. The function is not pub (it's a binary crate helper), so it can only be tested from within the same module. Integration tests in tests/ already cover end-to-end behavior.

reuse.how: Import and use as the parameter type for the new helper function. Construct in unit tests via its public fields.
Already imported in main.rs. Used in the match arm inside the helper.
Used in unit tests to construct test entries. Display impl formats as 'entity#constraint[index]' — the fallback label format.
Already assert on stdout content ('OK', 'VIOLATED', 'All constraints satisfied', 'Some constraints violated') and exit codes. Serve as regression tests for the refactoring — no modification needed.
